abstract: The political landscape in Slovakia has been significantly reshaped by the ascent of new parties leveraging anti-corruption, anti-establishment, and populist rhetoric. This shift has disrupted the traditional alternation between right-wing and left-wing blocs; however, the inherent heterogeneity of these new actors has not prevented the successful formation of governing coalitions or the ratification of formal agreements. In fact, Slovak governance has seen a stabilization of coalition patterns that increasingly align with the "ministerial government" model. These administrations rely on comprehensive, public coalition agreements that explicitly dictate office allocation, manage inter-party relations, and utilize a "coalition council" to resolve internal disputes. Interestingly, this institutional stabilization remained resilient through the global financial and migration crises—factors that had minimal impact on Slovakia’s democratic quality. Conversely, the COVID-19 pandemic served as a critical turning point, facilitating a temporary surge in prime ministerial dominance that disrupted the gradual consolidation of a more collaborative coalition governance model.
